conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you a few questions.

First, do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. I have a child - my 14-year-old, Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To help determine your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask:

Are you currently responsible for Alex? By this I mean, does Alex live with you and do you look after them?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives with me and I look after them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Next, I need to check your immigration and residence status.

Are you living in the UK, and do you have the right to live in the UK (for example, are you a British citizen, have settled status, or have the right to reside in the UK)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I don't live in the UK. I'm currently living abroad.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. Since you're living abroad, I need to understand a bit more about your situation.

Are you working abroad for an employer based in the UK or for an international organisation, or are you a Crown servant (such as a diplomat or member of the armed forces) posted overseas?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I'm not working for a UK-based employer or an international organisation, and I'm not a Crown servant posted overseas.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, I'm afraid you are **not eligible** for Child Benefit for Alex.

Here's why: While Alex is 14 years old (which meets the age requirement for Child Benefit - under 16, or under 20 if in approved education or training) and you are responsible for them and they live with you, the key issue is your residence status.

To claim Child Benefit, you must normally live in the UK. There are some exceptions for people living abroad, such as:
- Crown servants (diplomats, armed forces members) posted overseas
- People working for UK-based employers abroad
- People working for international organisations

Since you're living abroad and don't fall into any of these exception categories, you're not eligible to claim Child Benefit at this time.

If your circumstances change and you move back to the UK in the future, you would be able to apply for Child Benefit at that point, provided you continue to be responsible for Alex.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 14 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 14 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is fostered but the council is not paying for accommodation or maintenance
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK
circumstances: Claimant does not live in the UK
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child is fostered but the council is not paying for accommodation or maintenance
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 14 years old, which falls within the age eligibility for Child Benefit (under 16, or under 20 if in approved education or training). The claimant confirms that Alex lives with them and they are responsible for Alex. However, the claimant is living abroad and is not working for a UK-based employer, an international organisation, or serving as a Crown servant posted overseas. According to UK Child Benefit rules, you must normally live in the UK to be eligible for Child Benefit. There are exceptions for certain people working abroad (such as Crown servants or those working for UK employers/international organisations), but the claimant does not fall into any of these exception categories. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has a 14-year-old child named Alex who lives with them and for whom they are responsible. While the child meets the age requirements and the claimant is responsible for them, the claimant is living abroad and does not qualify for any of the exceptions that would allow them to claim Child Benefit while living outside the UK. As a result, they are not eligible for Child Benefit.
